author: jdegoes
body:
# Algebraic Patch/Diff System for ZIO Schema 2

## Overview

Implement a pure, algebraic patch/diff system for ZIO Schema 2 that represents structural changes as first-class, serializable data. The system provides a typed API (`Patch[A]`) built on an untyped core (`DynamicPatch`) that operates on `DynamicValue`.

Below is a rough outline or sketch of the design of patching & diffing, subject to revision by the implementor based on technical feasibility considerations.

## Core Architecture

### Type Hierarchy

```scala
// Typed patch (user-facing API)
case class Patch[A](dynamicPatch: DynamicPatch, schema: Schema[A]) {
  /** Apply patch with specified mode */
  def apply(value: A, mode: PatchMode = PatchMode.Strict): Either[SchemaError, A]
  
  /** Compose patches sequentially (monoid operation) */
  def ++(that: Patch[A]): Patch[A]
}

// Untyped patch (operates on DynamicValue)
case class DynamicPatch(ops: Vector[DynamicPatchOp]) {
  /** Apply to dynamic value */
  def apply(value: DynamicValue, mode: PatchMode): Either[SchemaError, DynamicValue]
  
  /** Compose patches */
  def ++(that: DynamicPatch): DynamicPatch
}
```

### Operations

```scala
case class DynamicPatchOp(optic: DynamicOptic, operation: Operation)

sealed trait Operation
object Operation {
  case class Set(value: DynamicValue) extends Operation
  case class PrimitiveDelta(op: PrimitiveOp) extends Operation
  case class SequenceEdit(ops: Vector[SeqOp]) extends Operation
  case class MapEdit(ops: Vector[MapOp]) extends Operation
}
```

## Typed API

All typed operations live in `Patch` companion object:

```scala
object Patch {
  /** Empty patch (monoid identity) */
  def empty[A](implicit schema: Schema[A]): Patch[A]
  
  /** Set a field/element to a value (clobber semantics) */
  def set[S, A](optic: Optic[S, A], value: A)(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  
  /** Increment numeric field */
  def increment[S](optic: Optic[S, Int], delta: Int)(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  def incrementLong[S](optic: Optic[S, Long], delta: Long)(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  def incrementDouble[S](optic: Optic[S, Double], delta: Double)(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  
  /** Edit string field */
  def editString[S](optic: Optic[S, String], edits: Vector[StringOp])(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  
  /** Sequence operations */
  def append[S, A](optic: Optic[S, Vector[A]], elements: Vector[A])(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  def insertAt[S, A](optic: Optic[S, Vector[A]], index: Int, elements: Vector[A])(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  def deleteAt[S, A](optic: Optic[S, Vector[A]], index: Int, count: Int)(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  
  /** Map operations */
  def addKey[S, K, V](optic: Optic[S, Map[K, V]], key: K, value: V)(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  def removeKey[S, K, V](optic: Optic[S, Map[K, V]], key: K)(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
  def modifyKey[S, K, V](optic: Optic[S, Map[K, V]], key: K, valuePatch: Patch[V])(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Patch[S]
}
```

## Primitive Operations

```scala
sealed trait PrimitiveOp
object PrimitiveOp {
  // Numeric deltas
  case class IntDelta(delta: Int) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class LongDelta(delta: Long) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class DoubleDelta(delta: Double) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class FloatDelta(delta: Float) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class ShortDelta(delta: Short) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class ByteDelta(delta: Byte) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class BigIntDelta(delta: BigInt) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class BigDecimalDelta(delta: BigDecimal) extends PrimitiveOp
  
  // String edits (LCS-based)
  case class StringEdit(ops: Vector[StringOp]) extends PrimitiveOp
  
  // Temporal deltas
  case class InstantDelta(duration: java.time.Duration) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class DurationDelta(duration: java.time.Duration) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class LocalDateDelta(period: java.time.Period) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class LocalDateTimeDelta(period: java.time.Period, duration: java.time.Duration) extends PrimitiveOp
  case class PeriodDelta(period: java.time.Period) extends PrimitiveOp
  // ... other temporal types
}

sealed trait StringOp
object StringOp {
  case class Insert(index: Int, text: String) extends StringOp
  case class Delete(index: Int, length: Int) extends StringOp
}
```

## Collection Operations

```scala
sealed trait SeqOp
object SeqOp {
  /** Insert at index (fails if index occupied in Strict mode) */
  case class Insert(index: Int, values: Vector[DynamicValue]) extends SeqOp
  
  /** Append to end (always succeeds) */
  case class Append(values: Vector[DynamicValue]) extends SeqOp
  
  /** Delete count elements starting at index */
  case class Delete(index: Int, count: Int) extends SeqOp
  
  /** Modify element at index with nested operation */
  case class Modify(index: Int, op: Operation) extends SeqOp
}

sealed trait MapOp
object MapOp {
  /** Add key-value (fails if key exists in Strict mode) */
  case class Add(key: DynamicValue, value: DynamicValue) extends MapOp
  
  /** Remove key (fails if key missing in Strict mode) */
  case class Remove(key: DynamicValue) extends MapOp
  
  /** Modify value at key with nested operation */
  case class Modify(key: DynamicValue, op: Operation) extends MapOp
}
```

## Patch Application Modes

```scala
sealed trait PatchMode
object PatchMode {
  /** Fail on precondition violations (e.g. modifying non-existent key) */
  case object Strict extends PatchMode
  
  /** Skip operations that fail preconditions */
  case object Lenient extends PatchMode
  
  /** Replace/overwrite on conflicts */
  case object Clobber extends PatchMode
}
```

## Schema Integration

```scala
trait Schema[A] {
  /** 
   * Compute smart patch from oldValue to newValue.
   * Uses heuristics to choose between delta/edit vs set operations.
   */
  def diff(oldValue: A, newValue: A): Patch[A]
  
  /** Convenience method - apply patch with Strict mode */
  def patch(value: A, patch: Patch[A]): Either[SchemaError, A] =
    patch.apply(value, PatchMode.Strict)
}
```

## Diffing Strategy

The `Schema#diff` implementation uses smart heuristics:

1. **New elements/keys**: Use `Operation.Set` 
   - Sequence insertions at new indices
   - Map additions for new keys
   - Sum type switches to different cases

2. **String modifications**: Use `StringEdit` if edit sequence is shorter than new string, otherwise use `Set`

3. **Numeric modifications**: Use delta operations (`IntDelta`, etc.)

4. **Structural modifications**: Recursively diff nested structures, using field-level patches for records

5. **Temporal modifications**: Use temporal delta operations for date/time types

## Laws

### Roundtrip Law
For all values, the following must hold:
```scala
∀ (schema: Schema[A], old: A, new: A).
  schema.diff(old, new).apply(old) == Right(new)
```

### Monoid Laws
```scala
// Identity (empty patch)
∀ (p: Patch[A]). 
  p ++ Patch.empty == p
  Patch.empty ++ p == p

// Associativity
∀ (p1: Patch[A], p2: Patch[A], p3: Patch[A]).
  (p1 ++ p2) ++ p3 == p1 ++ (p2 ++ p3)
```

### Serializability
All patch types are pure data (DynamicValue, DynamicOptic, primitives) and must be serializable:
```scala
∀ (p: Patch[A]). 
  jsonCodec.decode(jsonCodec.encode(p)) == Right(p)
```

## Implementation Notes

- `Operation.Set` uses `DynamicValue` to represent whole values for materialization cases
- `Patch[A]` converts typed values to `DynamicValue` via `Schema[A]` before applying `DynamicPatch` constructor
- String edits use LCS (Longest Common Subsequence) algorithm
- Sequence edits also use LCS for computing minimal insert/delete sequences

## Success Criteria

- [ ] `DynamicPatch` defined with all operation types, capable of transforming one DynamicValue to another in the most minimal possible way
- [ ] `Patch[A]` wraps `DynamicPatch` with an additional `Schema[A]`
- [ ] Typed API in `Patch` companion object for all common operations
- [ ] `Schema#diff` implements smart diffing strategy
- [ ] `PatchMode` controls patch application behavior
- [ ] All fallible operations return `Either[SchemaError, A]` so error information is preserved
- [ ] Roundtrip law holds for all schema types
- [ ] Monoid laws hold for patch composition
- [ ] All patch types serialize/deserialize correctly (except `Patch` itself, which cannot be serialized unless `Schema` is serialized, which in the general case requires a `TypeRegistry`)
- [ ] String diffs use LCS algorithm
- [ ] Sequence diffs use LCS algorithm
- [ ] Comprehensive tests for all operation types, and for serialization of non-`Patch` types such as DynamicPatch and everything it contains

## Example Usage

```scala
@schema 
@optics
case class Person(name: String, age: Int, address: Address)

@schema 
@optics
case class Address(street: String, city: String, country: String)

val old = Person("Alice", 30, Address("123 Main St", "NYC", "USA"))
val new1 = Person("Alice", 31, Address("456 Elm St", "NYC", "USA"))

// Automatic diffing
val patch1: Patch[Person] = Person.schema.diff(old, new1)
patch1(old) // Right(new1)

// Manual patch construction
val patch2: Patch[Person] = Patch.increment(Person.age, 1) ++ 
             Patch.set(Person.address(Address.street), "456 Elm St")
patch2(old) // Right(new1)

// Patch composition
val patch3 = patch1 ++ patch2
patch3(old) // Applies both patches sequentially

// Different application modes
patch2(old, PatchMode.Strict)   // Fails if preconditions violated
patch2(old, PatchMode.Lenient)  // Skips failed operations
patch2(old, PatchMode.Clobber)  // Replaces on conflicts

// Serialization (via @schema on DynamicPatch)
val json = jsonCodec.encode(patch1.dynamicPatch)
val recovered = jsonCodec.decode[DynamicPatch](json)
```
title: Finalize Patch & Diffing

body:
# Overview

This ticket involves adding derived optics support for Scala 2.13 and Scala 3.5+. Users will extend `DerivedOptics[T]` in their companion object to gain access to automatically-generated lenses and prisms via a typed `optics` accessor.

The mechanism is as follows:

- `DerivedOptics[T]`: Extend this trait in the companion object of a case class, sealed trait, or enum. Access optics via `Companion.optics.fieldName` which returns appropriately-typed `Lens` or `Prism` instances. An optional variant, `DerivedOptics_[T]`, prefixes all optic accessors with an underscore character (`_`) to avoid name collisions.

**Implementation Notes**:

1. The `optics` method returns a structurally-typed object. For case classes, each field becomes a `Lens` accessor. For sealed traits/enums, each variant becomes a `Prism` accessor (with the first letter lower-cased to avoid colliding with the type name).

2. The optics object **must be cached** to avoid repeated allocation. Users should not need to manually cache `val myOptics = Person.optics`—calling `Person.optics` multiple times must return the same cached instance.

3. For Scala 3, use `transparent inline def` to expose the structural refinement type at compile time. For Scala 2, use whitebox macros to achieve the same effect.

---

## Optics

### Case Class

#### User Code

```scala
final case class Person(name: String, age: Int)
object Person extends DerivedOptics[Person]
```

#### Usage

```scala
val nameLens: Lens[Person, String] = Person.optics.name
val ageLens: Lens[Person, Int] = Person.optics.age

val person = Person("Alice", 30)
nameLens.get(person)              // "Alice"
ageLens.replace(person, 31)       // Person("Alice", 31)
```

#### Structural Type

```scala
Person.optics  // : { def name: Lens[Person, String]; def age: Lens[Person, Int] }
```

---

### Sealed Trait

#### User Code

```scala
sealed trait Result[+A]
object Result extends DerivedOptics[Result[_]] {
  final case class Success[A](value: A) extends Result[A]
  object Success extends DerivedOptics[Success[_]]
  
  final case class Failure(error: String) extends Result[Nothing]
  object Failure extends DerivedOptics[Failure]
  
  case object Pending extends Result[Nothing]
}
```

#### Usage

```scala
// Prisms for the sealed trait variants
val successPrism: Prism[Result[Int], Success[Int]] = Result.optics.success
val failurePrism: Prism[Result[Nothing], Failure] = Result.optics.failure
val pendingPrism: Prism[Result[Nothing], Pending.type] = Result.optics.pending

// Lenses for case class fields
val valueLens: Lens[Success[Int], Int] = Success.optics.value
val errorLens: Lens[Failure, String] = Failure.optics.error

val result: Result[Int] = Success(42)
successPrism.getOption(result)    // Some(Success(42))
failurePrism.getOption(result)    // None
```

#### Structural Types

```scala
Result.optics   // : { def success[A]: Prism[Result[A], Success[A]]; def failure: Prism[Result[Nothing], Failure]; def pending: Prism[Result[Nothing], Pending.type] }
Success.optics  // : { def value: Lens[Success[A], A] }
Failure.optics  // : { def error: Lens[Failure, String] }
```

---

### Enum (Scala 3 only)

#### User Code

```scala
enum Status {
  case Active(since: Long)
  case Inactive
  case Suspended(reason: String, until: Long)
}
object Status extends DerivedOptics[Status]
```

#### Usage

```scala
// Prisms for enum cases
val activePrism: Prism[Status, Status.Active] = Status.optics.active
val inactivePrism: Prism[Status, Status.Inactive.type] = Status.optics.inactive
val suspendedPrism: Prism[Status, Status.Suspended] = Status.optics.suspended

val status: Status = Status.Active(1234567890L)
activePrism.getOption(status)     // Some(Active(1234567890L))
inactivePrism.getOption(status)   // None
```

#### Structural Type

```scala
Status.optics  // : { def active: Prism[Status, Active]; def inactive: Prism[Status, Inactive.type]; def suspended: Prism[Status, Suspended] }
```

---

## Definition of Done

This ticket is **not complete** until the following comprehensive test suites pass on both Scala 2.13 and Scala 3.5+.

### Lens Tests (Case Classes)

| Test Case | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `get field value` | `Person.optics.name.get(Person("Alice", 30))` returns `"Alice"` |
| `replace field value` | `Person.optics.name.replace(person, "Bob")` returns `Person("Bob", 30)` |
| `modify field value` | `Person.optics.age.modify(person, _ + 1)` returns `Person("Alice", 31)` |
| `compose lenses for nested access` | `Employee.optics.address.andThen(Address.optics.city)` works correctly |
| `lens for generic case class` | `Box[Int].optics.value` has type `Lens[Box[Int], Int]` |
| `lens for case class with many fields (10+)` | All fields accessible |
| `lens for fields with keyword names` | Fields named `` `type` ``, `` `class` ``, `` `val` `` work correctly |
| `lens for optional fields` | `Lens[T, Option[A]]` works correctly |
| `lens for collection fields` | `Lens[T, List[A]]` works correctly |
| `empty case class` | `Empty.optics` compiles (no accessors) |
| `single-field case class` | `Wrapper.optics.value` works |
| `case class with private constructor` | Optics still work |
| `case class extending trait` | Only case class fields become lenses |

### Prism Tests (Sealed Traits / Enums)

| Test Case | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `getOption returns Some for matching variant` | `Shape.optics.circle.getOption(Circle(5.0))` returns `Some(Circle(5.0))` |
| `getOption returns None for non-matching variant` | `Shape.optics.circle.getOption(Rectangle(3, 4))` returns `None` |
| `reverseGet constructs the variant` | `Shape.optics.circle.reverseGet(Circle(5.0))` returns `Circle(5.0)` typed as `Shape` |
| `prism for case object` | `Shape.optics.point.getOption(Point)` returns `Some(Point)` |
| `prism names are lower-cased` | Accessor is `circle`, not `Circle` |
| `nested sealed traits` | Prisms compose correctly |
| `enum simple cases` (Scala 3) | Parameterless enum cases work |
| `enum parameterized cases` (Scala 3) | Enum cases with fields work |
| `sealed trait with type parameters` | `Result.optics.success[Int]` returns `Prism[Result[Int], Success[Int]]` |

### Composition Tests

| Test Case | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `lens andThen lens` | Nested field access works |
| `prism andThen lens` | Access field inside a variant |
| `lens andThen prism` | Access variant inside a field |
| `prism andThen prism` | Nested variant access |

### Traversal Tests

| Test Case | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `traverse list field` | Combine lens with list traversal |
| `traverse option field` | Combine lens with optional |
| `traverse nested collections` | `List[Option[A]]` traversal |

### Caching Tests

| Test Case | Description |
|-----------|-------------|

author: ghostdogpr
body:
Let's say I define a neotype `DropRate` like this:
```scala
type DropRate = DropRate.Type
object DropRate extends Newtype[Double] {
  override inline def validate(input: Double): Boolean =
    input >= 0 && input <= 1
}
```

The `Schema` we get for `DropRate` is basically `Schema[Double]` because it's an opaque type, but I want to run the validation code to prevent constructing invalid values. So I added this given to wrap the `Double`:
```scala
inline given newTypeSchema[A, B](using newType: Newtype.WithType[A, B], schema: Schema[A]): Schema[B] =
  Schema.derived[B].wrap[A](newType.make, newType.unwrap)
```
However I get a runtime error when I try to construct a case class with a `DropRate` field.

```scala
case class Test(a: DropRate, b: String) derives Schema
val schema = Schema[Test].reflect.asRecord.get
val registers = Registers(schema.constructor.usedRegisters)

schema.registers(0).set(registers, RegisterOffset.Zero, DropRate(0.5))
schema.registers(1).set(registers, RegisterOffset.Zero, "test")
schema.constructor.construct(registers, RegisterOffset.Zero)
```
Fails with:
```
java.lang.ClassCastException: class java.lang.Double cannot be cast to class java.lang.String
```

The main difference is that when using the wrapper, the `register` for my field is `Object(0)` instead of `Double(0)`, because it's a wrapper and not a primitive. However the `construct` method doesn't seem to know about that.

Full reproducer:
```scala
import neotype.*
import zio.blocks.schema.*
import zio.blocks.schema.binding.*

inline given newTypeSchema[A, B](using newType: Newtype.WithType[A, B], schema: Schema[A]): Schema[B] =
  Schema.derived[B].wrap[A](newType.make, newType.unwrap)

type DropRate = DropRate.Type
object DropRate extends Newtype[Double] {
  override inline def validate(input: Double): Boolean =
    input >= 0 && input <= 1
}

case class Test(a: DropRate, b: String) derives Schema

@main
def run(): Unit = {
  val schema = Schema[Test].reflect.asRecord.get

  val registers = Registers(schema.constructor.usedRegisters)

  schema.registers(0).set(registers, RegisterOffset.Zero, DropRate(0.5))
  schema.registers(1).set(registers, RegisterOffset.Zero, "test")

  val res = schema.constructor.construct(registers, RegisterOffset.Zero)

  println(res)
}

```
title: Issue with construct when using wrapper + opaque type

author: jdegoes
body:
After thinking through #193 and related issues, I have come to the conclusion that we need both a new Reflect node and a new Binding type for this node.

```scala
  case class Wrapper[F[_, _], A, B](
    typeName: TypeName[A],   
    wrapped: Reflect[F, B],
    wrapperBinding: F[BindingType.Wrapper[A, B], A],
    doc: Doc = Doc.Empty,
    modifiers: Seq[Modifier.Wrapper] = Nil
  ) extends Reflect[F, B]
```

Note that a reflect wrapper takes a type `A` and wraps it into a type `B`, hence, a wrapper node extends `Reflect[F, B]`.

Let me walk through how this looks for different "wrapper" types:

- **`opaque type Age`**. For an opaque type `Age` that is actually an integer, the underlying `wrapped` is a primitive `Int`, while the outer `Wrapped` has reflect type `Age`, and the binding simply casts the `Int` to the `Age` or forwards to the `apply` constructor for `Age`.
- **`case class Email(value: String)`**. For a case class wrapper, the underlying wrapped is a `String`, while the outer reflect has type `Email`. The binding wraps the string to an Email by calling the Email constructor, optionally doing validation.

The binding for wrapper looks something like:

```scala
  final case class Wrapper[A, B](
    wrap: A => Either[ValidationError, B],
    unwrap: B => A,
    defaultValue: Option[() => B] = None,
    examples: collection.immutable.Seq[B] = Nil
  ) extends Binding[BindingType.Wrapper[A, B], B] {
```

This design provides a very flexible way to handle the differences between opaque types, new types, wrapper classes, and the like, acknowledging that some of these may have validation logic or custom constructors, some of which may fail.

We will need to carefully design `ValidationError` to be suitable for wrapper but also suitable for custom validation.
title: Add new node & binding type: wrapper

author: ghostdogpr
body:
Apart from annotations, the last thing I see missing to port my zio-schema 1 code to zio-schema 2 is better support for transformations.

The use cases I have for this are the following:
- using the schema of a different type with a bidirectional transformation
  - this is basically what the old `transform` was doing
- renaming a type or a field
  - i actually was doing this with annotations but maybe a transformation would be a better fit?
- adding a field to an existing schema
  - the old `transform` didn't support that and it was already quite manual and cumbersome to do it, but I wonder if it can be better?

Currently there is `Reflect#transform` but the API is complex to use as you have to implement `ReflectTransformer`, especially the binding part is difficult to modify (e.g. modify the constructor/deconstructor for a record)

author: ghostdogpr
body: Hi! I've been working on porting some code (protobuf read/write) I had from zio-schema 1 to 2 and have some feedback about `Registers`. 1. When fields have default values, they are omitted from protobuf binary. Some when I read a record, I end up with the values for n fields, and all the missing fields take default values. What I used to do with zio-schema 1 is to have a precomputed `Array[Any]` with default values and start with an `Array.copyOf` to setup a result array with all field default values, then replace those as I read the protobuf binary. Then finally I was calling `construct(Chunk.fromArray(...))` to construct my record. With `Registers` I have to loop over `registers` to set them all with default values, then override those that have actual values. The logic is similar but slower than a simple array copy. It would be nice if I could directly copy the `Registers` with default values into another `Registers` at a given offset. Or is there an even better way? 2. `registers` returns a collection of the parent type `Register`, so all calls to `get` or `set` trigger vtable lookups, which ends up being quite significant. Any idea to avoid that? Thanks!
title: Feedback on Registers

author: jdegoes
body:
In some domains, the user's application has a finite set of data types that may appear in serialization (e.g. banking). The user wishes to have generic code, which can interact in generic ways with any of these data types. Yet, which of these data types is not known in advance.

An example is a `Function` that has a `Schema` defined for the input type and a `Schema` for the output type. Although, depending on the specifics, it may be possible to store and serialize the `Function`, yet the "server" which receives the function does not know and cannot know how to make values of unknown types, even if they are chosen from a closed universe of types supported by the application.

To deal with these, ZIO Schema 2 will have support for a _type registry_. A type registry is essentially a type-safe map from ``TypeName[A]` to `Binding[T, A]`. This map allows you to look up the binding for any specified type name. For example, you could look up the binding for `myapp.Person` data type, where you would discover it is a `Binding.Record`, which allows you to construct and deconstruct values of type `Person`.

A type registry is just one of the features needed to allow useful serialization and generic use of schemas. Another feature is `binding` given a type registry. Or one could say, rebinding. 

In ZIO Schema 2, schemas are always bound: because that's the most useful type of schema. However, underlying `Schema` is a lower-level structure called `Reflect`, which is not necessarily bound. A reflect could be bound or unbound. An unbound reflect can be serialized and deserialized identically, because it stores no functions or anything else which is not pure simple data.

In order to fully support schema serialization, we must allow rebinding of a `Reflect` using a `TypeRegistry`, which would either fail or yield a `Schema`. The reason that rebinding would fail is because the type registry does not contain all types referenced in the `Reflect`.

The final aspect of schema serialization is the actual schema serialization. Here, I think we can instead rely on conversion **to** and **from*** `DynamicValue`. Because every codec must support `DynamicValue`, this allows you to serialize and deserialize `Reflect` in any possible format, such as JSON, Avro, BSON, etc.

Now, many possible different `DynamicValue` could encode a `Schema`, so I think we should choose an encoding that matches exactly JSON Schema. This way, when a `DynamicValue` is converted to JSON, we will have a JSON Schema already, with no conversion required.

JSON Schema supports use of `x-***` properties in order to encode metadata, which for us, can be things like `TypeName`.

Collectively, all of these changes will enable useful serialization of schemas, for generic data processing, and provide compatibility with JSON Schema.

## Summary of Changes

1. Introduce `TypeRegistry`, a trait:
    ```scala
    trait TypeRegistry { def lookup[A](typeName: TypeName[A]): Binding[?, A] }
    ```
2. Implement `TypeRegistry.Collection` a class with `add` and `remove` methods that implements `TypeRegistry` (in the future, someone could make one based on Java reflection). These methods have the obvious type signatures, e.g. `def add[A](typeName: TypeName[A], binding: Binding[?, A]): TypeRegistry`, etc.
3. Implement full test suite for `TypeRegistry.Colllection`.
4. Implement `TypeRegistry.empty` and `TypeRegistry.default` that have no bindings and that have bindings for all standard Scala types, respectively.
5. Implement `Reflect.rebind`, with type signature:
    ```scala
    sealed trait Reflect[F[_, _], A] {
      def rebind(typeRegistry: TypeRegistry): Either[RebindiError, Reflect.Bound[A]] = ???
    }
    ```
5. Implement `Reflect#toJsonSchema` and `Reflect.fromJsonSchema`. These encode everything but the binding (which is pure data) into a JSON Schema, with custom properties for things which JSON Schema does not support natively. Note that JSON Schema `const` can be used to encode tags for variants. The goal should be to ensure that for every Reflect `r`, `r.toJsonSchema.toJson` is a valid JSON Schema, and that, `Reflect.fromJsonSchema(DynamicValue.fromJson(jsonSchema)).rebind(typeRegistry).right.get`  gets you back to where you started, given a "big enough" type registry.
6. Implement full test suite for rebinding, schema conversion to and from DynamicValue.
7. Implement helpers such as `Schema#serialize: DynamicValue` and `Schema.deserialize(typeRegistry, dynamicValue)`, but also `Schema#toJsonSchema: String`, `Schema.fromJsonSchema(typeRegistry, jsonString)`.
title: Type Registry & Associated Machinery for Schema Serialization

author: jdegoes
body:
We need to add the following optics:

1. `At(index)`. This one works on sequences, and is effectively a type of Optional. You might be able to get it, you might be able to set it, and of course, you can fold over it.
2. `AtKey(key)`. This one works on maps, and is also a type of Optional.
3. `AtIndices(indices)`. This one is a traversal.
4. `AtKeys(keys)`. This one is a traversal.

I am not so sure we need (3) - (4) for now, but we definitely need (1) - (2).

We can support syntax like:

```scala
val firstAddress = $(_.addresses.at(0)) // at(index)

val contents = $(_.shoppingCart.atKey("fabc-dfs-d123-sjckd")) // atKey(key)
```

Note that `DynamicOptic` must gain corresponding support for these constructs, as it cannot be allowed to diverge from `Optic`.
title: Add At Optics

author: plokhotnyuk
body:
The `derives` keyword is not allowed for Scala 3 enumeration cases, so, currently, need to derive schemas for all cases explicitly:

```scala
enum Color(val rgb: Int) derives Schema:
  case Red           extends Color(0xff0000)
  case Green         extends Color(0x00ff00)
  case Blue          extends Color(0x0000ff)
  case Mix(mix: Int) extends Color(mix)

object Color {
  implicit val redSchema: Schema[Color.Red.type]     = Schema.derived
  implicit val greenSchema: Schema[Color.Green.type] = Schema.derived
  implicit val blueSchema: Schema[Color.Blue.type]   = Schema.derived

  object Mix {
    implicit val schema: Schema[Color.Mix] = Schema.derived
  }
}
```
title: Missing support of `derives` keyword when deriving schemas for Scala 3 enumeration cases
